subject: Architectural Review: 6 Structural Issues Found
body:
## Architectural Review — SquadDash (C#/WPF)

Six architectural problems identified. No code was changed.

---

### Finding 1 — MainWindow God Object (Critical)

**Problem:** `MainWindow.xaml.cs` is a single class with **28,213 lines**. It owns: UI event handling, prompt execution lifecycle, workspace management, agent roster, file watchers, conversation persistence, maintenance, loops, inbox, docking, screenshots, TTS/speech, documentation mode, theming, installation management, multi-instance activation, quick replies, push-to-talk, remote control, and dozens of timers and dispatcher callbacks. Comments like `// _activeToolName moved to PromptExecutionController` show a history of incomplete extractions.

**Affected files:** `MainWindow.xaml.cs`, all of `SquadDash` namespace.

**Fix:** Define an `IApplicationHost` interface for cross-cutting callbacks, reduce `MainWindow` to a WPF lifecycle shell, and delegate each subsystem (workspace, loops, maintenance, inbox, voice, docs) to owned service objects.

---

### Finding 2 — PromptExecutionController: 40+ Delegate Injection (Leaky Abstraction)

**Problem:** `PromptExecutionController`'s constructor takes approximately **40 `Func<>` and `Action<>` delegate parameters**, all provided by `MainWindow`. This is manual untyped dependency inversion — every wire leads back into `MainWindow`'s private members. The constructor is essentially illegible and impossible to test without a full `MainWindow`.

**Affected files:** `PromptExecutionController.cs`, `MainWindow.xaml.cs`.

**Fix:** Define typed interfaces (`IPromptBoxState`, `ITranscriptRenderSink`, `IWorkspaceContext`, `IAgentRosterView`) and pass those instead of anonymous closures. Dependencies become explicit contracts, testing becomes possible without a live `MainWindow`.

---

### Finding 3 — AI Prompt Engineering Strings Inside Infrastructure

**Problem:** `PromptExecutionController` contains multi-kilobyte hardcoded `const string` fields: `TurnSummaryInstruction`, `InboxMessageInstruction`, `QuickReplyInstruction`, `CoordinatorDelegationAccountabilityInstruction`. These are AI behavioural policy, not controller mechanics. They're invisible to prompt engineers, impossible to A/B test, and require a compile cycle to change.

**Affected files:** `PromptExecutionController.cs` (lines ~112–182).

**Fix:** Extract to a `PromptInstructionSet` record or loadable resource. Inject via `IPromptInstructionProvider`. Decouples prompt copy from execution logic.

---

### Finding 4 — No DI Container; All Services `new`ed in MainWindow

**Problem:** Every service is instantiated inline in `MainWindow`'s constructor:
```
private readonly ApplicationSettingsStore _settingsStore = new();
private readonly SquadTeamRosterLoader _teamRosterLoader = new();
private readonly SquadRoutingDocumentService _routingDocumentService = new();
// ... and many more
```
This makes it impossible to swap implementations, configure test vs. production environments, or reason about service lifetimes without reading all of `MainWindow`.

**Affected files:** `MainWindow.xaml.cs`, all `*Store`, `*Service`, `*Registry` types.

**Fix:** Introduce `Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection` (available in .NET 8+). Register services at `App.xaml.cs` startup. Test overrides become trivial registrations.

---

### Finding 5 — `SquadBridgePromptBuilder` is Static with Complex Domain Logic

**Problem:** `SquadBridgePromptBuilder` is `internal static class` containing regex-based NLP, team file reading, routing rule parsing, mention detection, and hiring-intent detection. Being static makes it untestable in isolation, undiscoverable as a dependency, and impossible to instrument separately.

**Affected files:** `SquadBridgePromptBuilder.cs`.

**Fix:** Convert to a normal class implementing `IPromptBuilder`. Inject workspace/routing data via constructor. Existing tests in `SquadBridgePromptBuilderTests.cs` can supply controlled fixtures rather than real file system state.

---

### Finding 6 — Panel Controllers Accept Raw WPF Elements; No ViewModel Boundary

**Problem:** `InboxPanelController`, `TasksPanelController`, `NotesPanelController`, and `MaintenancePanelController` all receive raw WPF types (`StackPanel`, `Border`, `TextBlock`, `WrapPanel`, `FlowDocumentScrollViewer`) in their constructors. These are code-behind in disguise — no view model, no data binding contract. Tests must either stub WPF objects or run under a WPF test context, which is expensive.

**Affected files:** `InboxPanelController.cs`, `TasksPanelController.cs`, `NotesPanelController.cs`, `MaintenancePanelController.cs`.

**Fix:** Introduce panel view models. Controllers populate view models; XAML binds to them. Core logic becomes testable without any WPF element.

---

### Summary Table

| # | Finding | Severity |
|---|---------|----------|
| 1 | MainWindow God Object (28 K lines) | Critical |
| 2 | PromptExecutionController 40+ delegate injection | High |
| 3 | AI prompt strings hardcoded in infrastructure | High |
| 4 | No DI container; all services `new`ed inline | High |
| 5 | Static `SquadBridgePromptBuilder` with domain logic | Medium |
| 6 | Panel controllers take raw WPF elements; no ViewModel | Medium |

The central theme: `MainWindow` became the integration hub for a codebase that otherwise has good single-responsibility instincts. The path forward is to complete the extraction already underway using interfaces, a DI container, and view models — rather than wiring everything through closures back to `MainWindow`.

actions.label: Start MainWindow decomposition
actions.prompt: Argus Weld's architectural review identified MainWindow.xaml.cs as a28,213-line God Object. Please start planning and implementing a decomposition: introduce an IApplicationHost interface for cross-cutting callbacks, extract each subsystem (workspace, inbox, maintenance, loop, voice/PTT, documentation) into dedicated owned service objects, and reduce MainWindow to a WPF lifecycle shell. Tackle one subsystem at a time on a feature branch. Files:SquadDash/MainWindow.xaml.cs, SquadDash/PromptExecutionController.cs.
actions.hint: Begin extracting subsystems from the28K-line MainWindow class

actions.label: Replace delegate injection with interfaces
actions.prompt: Argus Weld's architectural review found that PromptExecutionController takes ~40 Func<>/Action<> delegate parameters injected from MainWindow (SquadDash/PromptExecutionController.cs). Please refactor: define typed interfaces (IPromptBoxState, ITranscriptRenderSink, IWorkspaceContext, IAgentRosterView) and replace the anonymous closure wiring with proper interface injection. This is a prerequisite for testability of PromptExecutionController without a full MainWindow.
actions.hint: Fix the40-parameter constructor by introducing typed interfaces

actions.label: Introduce DI container
actions.prompt: Argus Weld's architectural review found that all services in SquadDash are instantiated with 'new' directly inside MainWindow (ApplicationSettingsStore, SquadTeamRosterLoader, SquadRoutingDocumentService, SquadInstallationStateService, SquadInstallerService, RunningInstanceRegistry, PushNotificationService, etc.). Please introduce Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection, register these services in App.xaml.cs, and inject them into MainWindow and other consumers. This will make test overrides trivial and service lifetimes explicit.
actions.hint: Add a DI container and stop using 'new' for services in MainWindow

actions.label: Extract prompt instruction strings
actions.prompt: Argus Weld's architectural review found that PromptExecutionController (SquadDash/PromptExecutionController.cs, lines ~112-182) contains multi-kilobyte hardcoded AI system prompt strings: TurnSummaryInstruction, InboxMessageInstruction, QuickReplyInstruction, CoordinatorDelegationAccountabilityInstruction. Please extract these to a PromptInstructionSet record and inject via an IPromptInstructionProvider interface so they can be changed without recompiling the controller.
actions.hint: Move AI system prompt strings out of the controller class

actions.label: Add to backlog
actions.prompt:
Please add the following architectural improvement tasks to the backlog (tasks.md or equivalent):
1. Decompose MainWindow God Object (28K lines) — introduce IApplicationHost, extract subsystems
2. ReplacePromptExecutionController's 40 delegate parameters with typed interfaces
3. Introduce Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection DI container for all services
4. Extract AI system prompt strings from PromptExecutionController to IPromptInstructionProvider
5. Convert static SquadBridgePromptBuilder to an injectable class implementing IPromptBuilder
6. Add ViewModel layer to panel controllers (Inbox, Tasks, Notes, Maintenance)
Source: Argus Weld architectural review, 2026-06-01.
actions.hint: Record all six findings as backlog items without starting work now
